artifacts.content:
Hi Carlos,

I’m hiding in my office for five quiet minutes before dismissal and wanted to actually write everything down for you instead of just mumbling “fine” when you ask how my day was.

The little pilots I’ve been doing at school are starting to add up. Since October 2024 I’ve been doing those 5–7 minute trauma‑informed movement breaks with Ms. Ana Delgado’s 7th‑grade homeroom during 3rd period. You know how Elena and the two kids who go to Lincoln Ridge Behavioral Health Center for anxiety kept ending up in the nurse’s office or getting written up for just shutting down? Walking a calm loop in the back hallway by the nurse’s office, plus some low‑stim stretches, has cut their nurse visits and discipline referrals way down. It’s such a small thing but it’s working.

And then there’s my own body yelling at me. After Dr. Patel at Advocate Christ Medical Center put me on that low‑dose beta blocker in May 2024 and told me I *have* to get 150 minutes of moderate exercise every week, I finally started the early‑morning “walk and talk” laps on the Lincoln Ridge track with staff twice a week. Honestly, it’s as much for my blood pressure as it is for “wellness culture.”

I keep thinking about how all of this ties into what we went through with Elena’s panic attacks around volleyball last year and your cardiology scare at Little Company of Mary back in summer 2021. We’re not a “sports family,” but movement is turning into this thread running through everything: my job, your long HVAC days, Mamá’s balance issues, even Mateo’s soccer and inhaler.

Can we sit down this weekend and map out our own walking schedule the way I do for the kids at school? If I can do it for 720 middle schoolers, surely I can do it for us.

Love you,
Linda

metadata.subject: Trying to practice what I preach

artifacts.content:
Hi Karen,

Catching you between crises, I hope. I wanted to loop you in on a few things that connect to your April 2024 small‑group check‑ins with our doubled‑up/homeless students.

You told me those kids in the 135th Street apartments said they can’t do running club or fitness club because of fees and no late bus. That’s still bothering me. I’ve set myself a goal of lining up at least $2,000 in quiet sponsorships by January 2025 (PTA + YMCA, maybe the hospital foundation) so those students can join without cost and get home safely. I’d like your help identifying who most needs it, but we’ll keep their names off any public lists.

Related: I’m also trying to clean up our discipline practices. After the February 2024 complaint about Coach Daniels making that 7th grader with ADHD walk silent laps for 30 minutes as punishment in the gym, I started drafting a building‑wide protocol that flat‑out bans using physical activity as discipline. The plan is to roll it out in August 2024 in‑service with concrete restorative strategies so teachers have something to do *instead* of taking away recess or movement.

On the more hopeful side, the lunchtime walking group I started in March 2025 for the three students with obesity‑related health plans from St. Mary’s pediatric endocrinology clinic is going well. We meet on the back field three days a week for 20 minutes, I log their steps and heart rates, and I keep a shared Google Sheet with you and Nurse Patel so we can watch for dizziness or blood sugar crashes, especially when their meds change.

Let’s grab 20 minutes next week to look at the sponsorship angle and the new protocol language together.

Gracias,
Linda

metadata.subject: Clubs access, Daniels incident, and walking group updates

artifacts.content:
Personal reflection – PE/PA goals, connections to family

I keep realizing how much my “professional” goals are just our family story rewritten for 720 kids.

Elena: October 2022 504 plan at Lincoln Ridge Pediatric Associates with Dr. Nguyen for exercise‑induced asthma; March 2023 panic attacks tied to 7th‑grade volleyball performance anxiety; February 2024 sessions with Dr. Ruiz at Lincoln Ridge Family Counseling to manage pre‑PE anxiety spikes. All of that pushed me to:
- Pilot low‑intensity indoor walking in the auxiliary gym November–February (20–25 minutes so kids like her can move without triggering symptoms).
- Build a lunchtime mindfulness‑and‑movement group in the aux gym for 2024–25 (short yoga + breathing, modeled on what Dr. Ruiz taught her).
- Push PE to label walking laps, breathing drills, and low‑impact circuits explicitly as stress‑management tools, with at least 10 minutes per class, three days a week.

Mateo: February 2024 broken left ankle at Brookside (six weeks in a cast at Silver Cross Hospital) and then being called “too slow” and excluded from recess soccer in spring 2024. That’s the emotional fuel behind the 8th‑grade Student Assistant Coach program and the requirement that student leaders get explicit training on inclusion and adapting games for kids with temporary injuries.

Mamá: March 2024 cardiology follow‑up at Little Company of Mary Hospital, A1C back in prediabetic range; September 2024 start of fall‑risk PT at Lincoln Ridge Outpatient Rehab after her minor fall by Brookside in March 2023. Her 20‑minute evening walking loop with me and the balance drills from her home exercise program are literally becoming curriculum for Family Movement Night and a daily walking option on the track.

All of this makes the work feel heavier and more urgent—but also clearer.

metadata.title: Family threads behind PE & activity goals

artifacts.content:
Event: Lunchtime adaptive movement blocks – personal schedule notes

Time: Recurring
- Mon/Wed 11:35–11:55 – 6th‑grade adaptive yoga (library makerspace)
- Tue/Thu/Fri 11:35–11:55 – St. Mary’s walking group (back field)

Details:

Mon/Wed – Adaptive yoga & breathing
Location: Library makerspace, Lincoln Ridge MS
Attendees: Five 6th‑graders with 504s for panic attacks during PE
Notes: Per 504 meetings on January 10 and 17, 2025 in the conference room by my office with Dr. Karen Lee, parents, and PE staff, these students are exempt from the cardiovascular fitness test. Instead, they complete a 15‑minute adaptive yoga sequence twice a week. I supervise, track minutes and incidents in a spreadsheet (names + brief incident history), and send monthly summaries to Dr. Lee.

Tue/Thu/Fri – Lunchtime walking group
Location: Back field behind Lincoln Ridge MS
Attendees: Three students with obesity‑related health plans from St. Mary’s pediatric endocrinology clinic
Notes: Started March 2025 as part of inclusive, non‑competitive options. We walk for 20 minutes, three days a week. I carry pedometers and a simple heart‑rate monitor, log individual step counts and heart rates in a shared Google Sheet (names, homerooms, med changes). Coordinate with Nurse Patel about any dizziness or blood sugar crashes after walking.

General reminders:
- Trauma‑informed approach: low‑stim environment, clear start/stop cues, students can opt to sit out a pose or lap without penalty.
- Protect confidentiality: close door blinds in makerspace; no photos on field.
- Use these groups as informal feedback loops for broader PE policy changes (no PA as punishment, more low‑impact options).

Diego,

I needed to write to someone who gets both the family side and the emergency‑response side of things.

First, you. When you told me about that mass‑casualty call in Chicago last October and how the sirens still set off your PTSD episodes, I heard echoes of what I’m seeing at school. During our fall 2023 lockdown drill in the auxiliary gym, one of my 7th‑graders, J., had a severe panic response—shaking, hyperventilating, totally checked out. It looked so much like what you described. That’s why I’ve made trauma‑informed PE a major goal for 2024–25: fewer sudden loud whistles, clearer pre‑briefing before drills, and quiet corners kids can use without being labeled “defiant.”

On top of that, I keep thinking about the October 2022 OCR case at Brookside, when I had to help file a bullying/disability‑accommodation complaint for that 6th‑grader with asthma and generalized anxiety who was being forced to run the full mile in under 10 minutes. The settlement meeting at the District 12 central office was awful, but it pushed me to bake individualized fitness goals and walking/low‑impact alternatives into our middle school PE program.

And then there’s me: my March 2023 meniscus surgery at Northwestern Memorial, the way my knee still blows up after a full 55‑minute supervision block in the main gym. Feeling that pain in my own body makes it impossible to ignore what we’re asking of kids with chronic joint issues.

Sometimes it feels like everything—your calls, Mamá’s fall on the cracked sidewalk by Brookside in March 2023, Mateo’s broken ankle at Silver Cross last February—is one long argument for slowing down, listening to bodies, and not treating “toughing it out” as a virtue.

How do you carry all of this without burning out completely? I’m trying to figure out my version of that.

Te quiero,
Linda

metadata.subject: Why I’m rethinking how we do PE

artifacts.content:
Family chat – Morales/Alvarez

Linda: Quick update before I forget – I finally emailed Councilwoman Reyes again about the sidewalk outside Brookside where Mamá fell back in March 2023 walking Mateo. I’m pushing Public Works this fall (Sept–Nov 2024) to fix the cracks and add another crosswalk near 127th so families can actually do a 10–15 minute walk to school without risking their ankles.

Rosa: Ay mija, no te preocupes tanto. I’m fine.

Linda: You *say* that, but then March 2024 your cardiologist at Little Company of Mary tells you your A1C is back in the prediabetic range. Our 20‑minute evening loop around Lincoln Ridge is non‑negotiable now. 😅

Carlos: She’s the Wellness Committee Chair even at home.

Linda: Speaking of, I’m stealing Mamá’s fall‑risk PT exercises from Lincoln Ridge Outpatient Rehab (the ones she started in September 2024) for a low‑impact station at our spring 2025 Family Movement Night in the LRMS gym. I already told the Spanish‑language WhatsApp group I’ll demo the exact balance drills her therapist gave her so abuelitos who’ve had falls can participate with the grandkids.

Diego: That’s actually brilliant. My patients never use the handouts they get.

Linda: Also trying to sort out Elena’s stuff. Between her October 2022 504 for exercise‑induced asthma, the March 2023 panic attacks around volleyball, and now Dr. Ruiz at Lincoln Ridge Family Counseling showing us those yoga/breathing sequences in Feb 2024, I’m planning a lunchtime mindfulness‑and‑movement group in the aux gym next year.

Rosa: Pobrecita, pero you’re doing everything you can.

Linda: I hope so. Anyway, who’s free for a Sunday walk on the school track? We can practice Mamá’s balance drills and let Mateo kick a ball around (carefully, no more broken ankles please).

Hi Principal Harris,

I wanted to share some context as we finalize next year’s wellness and PE priorities.

Over the past two years, I’ve seen how our written policy that says we “do not use or withhold physical activity as punishment” isn’t consistently lived out. The February 2024 incident where Coach Daniels had a 7th grader with ADHD walk silent laps for 30 minutes during PE as discipline was a turning point for me. I’m drafting a clear building‑wide protocol for August 2024 in‑service that bans using activity *or* removal of activity as punishment and instead trains staff in restorative conversations and positive behavior supports.

At the same time, we’re building more inclusive options. Since October 2024 I’ve been informally piloting 5–7 minute trauma‑informed movement breaks with Ms. Ana Delgado’s 7th‑grade homeroom during 3rd period. That group includes my daughter Elena and two students who receive counseling at Lincoln Ridge Behavioral Health Center for anxiety. A simple low‑stim stretch/walk circuit in the back hallway near the nurse’s office has noticeably reduced their nurse visits and discipline referrals for shutdown behaviors.

On the PE side, I’m working with Jason and Rachel to embed at least 10 minutes per class, three days a week, of explicitly labeled stress‑management activities—walking laps, breathing drills, low‑impact circuits—so students see movement as a tool for managing panic and anxiety, not just for sports performance. This grew directly out of Elena’s March 2023 panic attacks tied to volleyball and her pediatrician at Advocate Christ recommending daily light cardio and yoga.

I’ll bring draft language and sample lesson structures to our next admin meeting so we can align this with district expectations.

Best,
Linda

metadata.subject: Aligning discipline, movement breaks, and PE stress-management goals
